DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-18-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Pete Clancy (925-835-7500; pete@clancylawgroup.com) of Clancy & Diaz LLP for Plaintiff.
Michelle Younkin (415-278-1438; myounkin@daviswanglaw.com) of Davis Wang for Defendant.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT MERCY HOUSING MANAGEMENT GROUP, INC.'s DEMURRER to COMPLAINT:

Defendant Mercy Housing's demurrer to the second cause of action for "negligent hiring, training and retaining" is overruled.

The allegations against Mercy Housing on the basis of negligent hiring are not a separate cause of action, but part of Plaintiff's cause of action for negligence. As to Mercy Housing, negligence is alleged on the basis of both negligent hiring and respondeat superior vicarious liability for Mr. Taylor's actions. These are separate bases of liability. "Of course, even if [the defendant] is not, as a matter of law, vicariously liable under a respondeat superior theory, it is still possible that it could be directly liable for its own conduct in negligently hiring or supervising." (Z.V. v. County of Riverside (2015) 238 Cal.App.4th 889, 902.) "Ordinarily, a general demurrer may not be sustained, nor a motion for judgment on the pleadings granted, as to a portion of a cause of action." (Daniels v. Select Portfolio Servicing, Inc. (2016) 246 Cal.App.4th 1150, 1167.) In any event, "California case law recognizes the theory that an employer can be liable to a third person for negligently hiring, supervising, or retaining an unfit employee." (Doe v. Capital Cities (1996) 50 Cal.App.4th 1038, 1054.) Plaintiff adequately alleges that Mercy Housing hired and retained Mr. Taylor although it knew or through reasonable diligence should have known that he was unqualified for the position and posed a danger to the community.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-18-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Pete Clancy (925-835-7500; pete@clancylawgroup.com) of Clancy & Diaz LLP for Plaintiff.
Michelle Younkin (415-278-1438; myounkin@daviswanglaw.com) of Davis Wang for Defendant.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT MERCY HOUSING MANAGEMENT GROUP, INC.'s MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT:

Defendant Mercy Housing's motion to strike the complaint's prayer for punitive damages is granted with leave to amend.

"An employer shall not be liable for [exemplary] damages . . . based upon acts of an employee of the employer, unless the employer had advance knowledge of the unfitness of the employee and employed him or her with a conscious disregard of the rights or safety of others or authorized or ratified the wrongful conduct for which the damages are awarded or was personally guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. With respect to a corporate employer, the advance knowledge and conscious disregard, authorization, ratification or act of oppression, fraud, or malice must be on the part of an officer, director, or managing agent of the corporation." (Civ. Code, sec. 3294.)

Plaintiff fails to allege facts that entitle it to punitive damages against Defendant Mercy Housing. "[T]he concept assumes that such individual was acting in a corporate or employment capacity when the conduct giving rise to the punitive damages claim against the employer occurred . . . No purpose would be served by punishing the employer for an employee's conduct that is wholly unrelated to its business or to the employee's duties therein. We cannot conceive that Civil Code section 3294, subdivision (b) intended such a result." (College Hosp. Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 723-724.) There are no allegations of advance knowledge. There are no allegations that an officer, director, or managing agent of Mercy Housing was personally guilty of fraud, oppression, or malice. Finally, Plaintiff's conclusory allegation that Mercy Housing is liable because of its unspecified "post-incident ratification" is insufficient. "In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements as stated in . . . Civil Code section 3294" and must "state facts sufficient to support allegations." (Turman v. Turning Point of Central California, Inc. (2010) 191 Cal.App.4th 53, 63.) There are insufficient factual allegations that Defendant Mercy Housing ratified Mr. Taylor's actions. (See College Hosp., Inc., 8 Cal.4th at 726 ["For purposes of determining an employer's liability for punitive damages, ratification generally occurs where, under the particular circumstances, the employer demonstrates an intent to adopt or approve oppressive, fraudulent, or malicious behavior by an employee in the performance of his job duties]; Cruz v. HomeBase (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 160, 168 [same].)

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.
